ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for packaged Chinese herbal tea with less sugar; closest match is the general herbal tea beverage class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FHR covers tea and herbal infusions broadly; no specific code exists for packaged Chinese herbal tea with reduced sugar.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for packaged Chinese herbal tea with less sugar; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: herbal extracts (e.g. chrysanthemum, honeysuckle, licorice root)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one 300 ml can or bottle, which is the standard retail size for packaged Chinese herbal tea in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese Herbal Tea (Less Sugar) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chinese herbal tea (less sugar, packaged), cr_id D05010008. All per-100g values are exact matches from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2008 — sugar-sweetened beverages and lightly sweetened tea analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged Chinese herbal tea with less sugar. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on analogy with lightly sweetened tea beverages (sucrose-based, ~5–6g sugar/100ml) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 300 ml serving calculated as: GI (52) × available carbohydrate per serving (18.0g from 300g × 5.94g/100g) ÷ 100 = 9.4 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar at 5.74g/100g exceeds the 5g/100g beverage sugar threshold; independent blood pressure risk from sugar-sweetened beverages despite very low sodium (5mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage and choose the no-sugar variant instead, as dietary sugar independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, even when sodium is low.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 5.74g added sugar per 100g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar raises postprandial glucose rapidly
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage as it is a sugar-sweetened packaged drink with added sugar and no fibre, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation; the no-sugar variant is the recommended alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased cardiovascular risk through dyslipidaemia, weight gain, and insulin resistance; sodium is negligible at 5mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage, as habitual SSB consumption is linked to elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; the no-sugar variant is preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese herbal tea (less sugar, packaged) is a ready-to-drink commercially produced beverage popular in Singapore and across Chinese communities in Southeast Asia. It is brewed from traditional Chinese medicinal herbs such as chrysanthemum, honeysuckle, licorice root, and other cooling herbs, then lightly sweetened with sugar and packaged in cans or bottles for retail sale. The beverage has a mild, slightly bitter, and subtly sweet herbal flavour with a thin, watery texture. It is consumed for its perceived cooling and detoxifying properties in traditional Chinese medicine (TCM), particularly during hot weather or after consuming heaty foods. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie beverage at 24 kcal per 100g, with negligible protein (0.08g/100g) and zero fat. The carbohydrate content is 5.94g/100g, of which 5.74g is sugar, making it a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) despite the less-sugar label. Dietary fibre is absent and sodium is very low at 5mg/100g. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its added sugar content and zero fibre, which causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation. It also raises concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to the independent blood pressure and cardiovascular risk associated with regular SSB consumption. The no-sugar variant is the recommended alternative for health-conscious consumers.

Can I drink packaged herbal tea if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in a can of Chinese herbal tea less sugar?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010008), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from the HPB record. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, which is the approved GI source.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (D05010008); this packaged beverage contains no fat by formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; this is a clear liquid herbal beverage with no fibre. iron_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this packaged Chinese herbal tea product are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D05010008. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on lightly sweetened tea beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in approved sources. GL per serving calculated for a 300g (300ml) serving: 52 × (5.94 × 3.0) ÷ 100 = 9.4.
